article_title: A homelab disaster taught me why simple backups are better than complicated redundancy
core_thesis: The author recounts a personal homelab disaster where a NAS update caused path mapping changes, leading to accidental deletion of virtual machines and Docker containers. The incident underscores that RAID is not a backup, and that procrastinating on simple, off-site backups can result in hours of recovery effort. The author advocates for using Duplicati with encrypted cloud storage (Google Drive) as a quick, effective backup solution that provides peace of mind and is far simpler than relying on complex redundancy alone.
detailed_summary: The author describes a homelab setup where Proxmox servers stored VMs on a NAS for high availability. After a NAS update changed NFS mount paths, the author attempted to fix the issue but accidentally deleted the VMs and backups stored on the NAS. Despite knowing that RAID is not a backup, the author had not implemented any off-site backup strategy. The loss was limited to Home Assistant and a homelab dashboard, but the experience was a wake-up call. The author then set up Duplicati to back up encrypted blobs to Google Drive (5TB available via Google AI Pro subscription). The configuration took less than five minutes per system, with daily scheduled backups using 250MB chunks. The author emphasizes that this simple solution would have prevented hours of recovery effort and now provides significant peace of mind, even if Google cancels the account (the encrypted blobs can be redirected to another provider like BackBlaze B2). The article concludes with a strong recommendation to not procrastinate on homelab maintenance tasks.

entities.name: Patrick Campanale
entities.role: Author and homelab owner who experienced the data loss and wrote the article.

entities.name: Proxmox
entities.role: Hypervisor used to run virtual machines stored on the NAS.

entities.name: NAS
entities.role: Network Attached Storage that hosted VM files and backups; the source of the path mapping issue.

entities.name: Duplicati
entities.role: Open-source backup tool chosen for its simplicity and native Google Drive integration.

entities.name: Google Drive
entities.role: Cloud storage destination for encrypted backups, leveraging the author's existing 5TB plan.

entities.name: Home Assistant
entities.role: One of the lost VMs; easily recreated.

entities.name: BackBlaze B2
entities.role: Alternative cloud backup provider considered but not used due to existing Google Drive subscription.

key_data_points: RAID is not a backup; it only protects against drive failure, not accidental deletion or corruption (🟢 relevant to your homelab: relying on RAID alone is a common mistake that can lead to data loss).

A NAS update changed NFS mount paths, causing Proxmox to lose visibility of VMs; the author accidentally deleted the VMs while trying to fix the path mapping (🟢 relevant to your Red Teaming: understanding how configuration changes can cause cascading failures is critical for both defense and exploitation).

The author had been procrastinating on setting up off-site backups for months, which directly led to the data loss (🟢 relevant to your productivity: this highlights the importance of prioritizing maintenance tasks to avoid catastrophic downtime).

Duplicati was configured in under 5 minutes per system, using encryption, 250MB blob sizes, and daily 1 AM backups to Google Drive (🟢 relevant to your homelab: Duplicati is a lightweight, easy-to-setup backup tool that integrates with multiple cloud providers).

The author had 5TB of Google Drive storage via Google AI Pro subscription, making it a cost-effective backup destination (🟢 relevant to your student budget: leveraging existing subscriptions can reduce costs).

Duplicati encrypts backup blobs before uploading, so even if Google cancels the account, the data remains secure and can be redirected to another provider (🟢 relevant to your security: encryption ensures data confidentiality even when stored on third-party services).
methodology_steps: Identify the backup destination: Use an existing cloud storage subscription (e.g., Google Drive) or a paid service like BackBlaze B2 ($6/TB/month).
Choose a backup tool: Install Duplicati on each system that needs backup (Docker servers, VMs, etc.).
Configure Duplicati: Set a backup name, encryption password, and select the cloud storage destination (e.g., Google Drive).
Set backup parameters: Use 250MB blob sizes and schedule backups to run daily at 1 AM (or any convenient time).
Verify the backup: Ensure the first backup completes successfully and test restoration by reinstalling Duplicati on a test system and restoring from the encrypted blobs.
Repeat for all systems: Apply the same configuration to every server or VM that requires protection.
conclusions: Simple, encrypted off-site backups are far more effective than complex redundancy alone; the author's experience proves that a 5-minute setup can save hours of recovery time. This is directly applicable to homelab and production environments where data integrity is critical.

Procrastination on homelab maintenance tasks can lead to significant data loss; the author urges readers to prioritize backup setup immediately. For a student or ambitious homelabber, this is a low-effort, high-impact task that prevents future headaches.

Duplicati with Google Drive provides a cost-effective, secure backup solution that is easy to configure and maintain. The encryption ensures data privacy even on third-party cloud storage, making it suitable for sensitive homelab data.
personal_relevance_summary: This article is highly relevant to your homelab and DevOps interests, as it provides a real-world cautionary tale about the dangers of relying solely on RAID and procrastinating on backups. The step-by-step Duplicati setup is a practical, low-effort solution you can implement immediately to protect your own VMs and Docker containers. As a student, the cost-effective use of existing Google Drive storage aligns with budget constraints. The article also reinforces the importance of simple, automated maintenance—a lesson that applies to both homelab and cybersecurity practices, where redundancy without proper backups can lead to catastrophic data loss.
extra_advice: Consider automating backup verification by periodically restoring a small test file from your Duplicati backups to ensure the encryption password and blob integrity are intact. Additionally, explore using Duplicati's built-in backup verification feature or script a simple cron job that checks the backup status. This proactive step will prevent the 'backup that never worked' scenario and give you true confidence in your recovery plan.
